abo, interj. (U), 1. used by women in conjunction with names, praise names associated with various clans and the word ‘baba’ (q.v.), as a mild form of oath or to express surprise, consterna- tion, or indignation; ~ Angarīi aitù, kaï ndi ngombo!, good heavens, by the community of my clan Angari, am I a slave?; ~ baba, by my father, I will not go! 2. used with the foll. forms of clan names: Angari, ~ Anjirŭ, ~ Njirŭ;, Aceera, ~ Njeeri; Agacikŭ, ~ Ambŭi, ~ Mbūi; Ethaga, ~ Mbura; Aithi- randù, ~ Tthīra; Angŭi, ~ Ngūi; Aicakamŭyŭ, ~ ~ Irıimů.

March to May: mbura-ya- njahī, můringo, kīhu, gīïthananwa, műgira-njara. 3. June to August: gathaatň, muoria-nyoni, gaciciŭù, gathano, (N.K.) gathano-komŭ. 4. September to December: mbura-ya-mwere, mů- ringo, drera-hŭngŭù, kanya-hŭngŭ, kīya-kia-mŭhīa (mwere), kaya- karongo, kīhu, gīthananwa, můratho, njakoira, thano, themithŭ, gatumu (tumu); cf. kîmera, hīndī, kahinda.

křama', ci-, n. (1), those set apart as a council, council of elders; council, tribunal, association, party, com- mittee; a deliberative, advisory, legis- lative, judicial, or executive body in a country, province, district, ridge, or village; the graduated system of eldership for which Kikuyu married men become eligible; ~ ga kamatimů, the first grade of elders composed of recently married men with at least one child (admitted on payment of a goat or sheep, and carrying spears instead of staves of offce, who serve as assistants in the court as part of their training but are not entitled to judge a case, though they provide the core of the army); see njama ya ita; cf. (N.K.) ~ kīa mbiri imwe, ~ kīrīa kīnyinyi, ~ kīa lower degree of the ‘kiama’ proper (carrying unblackened staff and leaves loosely tied); ~ kīa matathi, also ~ kīrīa ~ gīa athamaki, ~ kīrīa gīkűrű, ~ kīa njoho, ~ kīa bûürüri, (Ndia) ~ gřa ituīro, ‘kiama’ proper, council of senior elders (the highest territorial body with judicial and ad- ministrative functions, composed of those who possess initiated children, carrying a blackened staff, as symbol of office see matathi); ~ kīa maturanguru, the inner circle of the council of senior elders with the highest religious and political au- thority, carrying ‘maturangurv’ (q.v.) and wearing ‘icùh? (q.v.), also (N.K.) ~ gia kīrogo (because of their power to put a curse on any one who persists in defying their decisions or orders); ~ kīa ihiga, tribunal court, tribunal; ~ gīa atumia, women’s guild; ~ kīia Barűŭthi (Ngabana), Governor’s Exe- cutive Council, also Legislative Coun- cil; ~ kīa mbūůkīra, an emergency council; ~ kīa mübira, football league; ~ kīa ndundu, secret or private council, confidential committee, inner circle; ~ gīa village council; ~ kīa rūgongo, ridge or location coun- cil; ~ kīa mwaki, council of a fire- linked unit or group of villages; ~ kīa bürüri, inter-district or country-wide council; ~ kīa a political party or association; ~ kīa aruti a wīra, trade union; ~ kīa endia a irio, food- sellers’ co-operative society; ~ gīa 2 kanitha, the governing body of a church. mükīiama, a-, n, member of the council of elders; member of any council or committee,

mwendia-rūhiŭ, mī-, n. (I-11), 1. man who contracts a matrilocal, matrilineal marriage; one who cannot pay customary ‘bride-price? through poverty but attaches himself to the family of a man with many daughters, one of whom he is allowed to treat as wife (he remains there and works for his father-in-law, the children belong to their maternal grandfather and his lineage, the eldest son being named after a maternal grandparent. Such marriage is always convertible into the normal patrilocal, patrilineal form by subsequent payment of bride-price). 2. unattached man who cohabits with a widow or, by permission, with a married woman. 3. one who helps a woman to clear herself of ‘unclean- ness’ due to the death of her husband by having sexual intercourse with her, unless this is done by the younger brother who inherits her; cf. mū- thambania. 4. stranger willing to per- form ceremonial coition with a woman on one of the occasions prescribed by custom, e.g. if she bears twins or has a child which cuts the upper teeth first, or, in the case of a newly ini- tiated girl, at the conclusion of the aggregation rites; see mbiro ya rūenji.

wendo, mawendo, n. (1), love, affec- tion, charity; liking, desire; ~ ndūrī guoya, love fears nothing; ~ wa mwana Üükīrite muoyo, love for one’s child is greater than love for one’s life; cf. wendwa, wendani. kīendo, ci-, n. desire, liking, inclination. rūendo, nyendo, n. strong affection, desire, passion; mwana wa ~, a favourite child. ~ nī rűrīaga, too much love destroys. [enda]

Mau-mau?, n. (1), name given to a reactionary organization among the Kikuyu, anti-European, anti-Chris- tian, which after working covertly for a time established its influence over the majority of the people; after diffi- cult and prolonged operations by the Government its gangs were reduced and dissipated, and by the end of 1956 only a handful remained at large; the name is said to have been first applied towards the end of the nineteenth century to a band of young men who terrorized a locality in the Kiambu district; see mau-mau’,

mütuithania!, a-, n, (1v), arbitrator, one who decides between two dis- putants. [tua!]

ituika, (ma-), n. (111), a handing over or ceremonial transfer of responsi- bility from one generation-set to another; in addition to the annual circumcision or initiatory age-sets, (see marika ma kahiŭ) there were generation-sets (also called marika) each covering a period of thirty years or more, during which the duly quali- fied representatives were in authority and responsible for the conduct of public affairs; the transfer of responsi- bility involved the contribution of a goat or sheep from every accountable member of the incoming generation towards the redemption of the country from the outgoing set (see kŭŭra bürüri); this process took many years. The best remembered of these ruling generation-sets, in receding order, are: Mwangi, Maina, Iregi, Ndemi, Mathathi, Ciïira, Cuma. The ‘Maina’ generation handed over to the ‘Mwang? about 1900; the latest, the ‘Trůngŭ’ or ‘Maina Kanyi’ set, which is said to have been preparing to suc- ceed the ‘Mwangi’ sometime between 1925-32, was prevented by the social and political changes brought about by europeanizing influences and by prohibition of the ceremonies by Government. [tua1]

gītuïkano, i-, n. (111), separation, breaking up of herds; break-up of a fellowship, companionship; ~ kīao kïonekire o maarña, the breach between them became apparent when they fought. [tua']

With the prefixes of cl. 5, 15b, and 16 there are special meanings: 1. cl. 5, rī~, now; o rī~, here and now, just a few moments ago; o na rī~, even now, certainly; kîrī~, see sep. entry kīriu. 2. cl. 15b; ki~, there; thit na kū~, get away with you; gūtiri mündŭ, there is nobody there; gīkñ~, after the manner of that place, local. 3. cl. 16, ha~, just there, thereabouts; thiï na ha~, go over there, in that direction; kīrī ha~, (an evasive reply, when repeatedly asked where sth. is, to close the matter) it is somewhere there; one nī ha~ haranduura, that’s where I feel the pain.

w-, may represent -the concord prefix of cl. 14, when foll. by a vowel; in such cases the nouns must be looked for as usual under the initial letter of the stem.

wa / waa, interj. (U), expr. surprise, amazement, mockery, derision.

-wa, suffix (U), contracted form of poss. a. ref. to Ist pers., used with a few n. of cl. 1/2, written conjunctively; my relative; see -akwa.

-wa, part. (U), invariable, preceded by ni, no, ti, to written conjunctively, foll. by demonstratives of all classes, only at the beginning of a sentence, often indicating a sense of surprise or relief after a period of anticipation; nī~ gīkī, this is it (what we have been looking for).

wa-, an invariable extra prefix found with certain words esp. the names of animals in stories often associated with cl. 1/2 concords; it may be fol- lowed by the normal class prefix.

ya, interj. (U), indic. surprise or in- credulity.

kiya, n. cl. 7 (1v), cold, misty weather during the short rains; see mwaka. | mwia, mīi-, n. (1v), 1. swift current, deep channel in a river; cf. 2. arm, branch, between islets; islet in stream; cf. gîcigirira, gīíthama.

kaya-karongo / kea, n. (11-111), (N.K.) time of year usu. around February (when at higher altitudes slight frosts may blight the sweet- potato vines); cf. kīya-kīa-mwere; see mwaka.

kīya-kīa-mwere, n. (1-U-I1), season of year when millet has to be pro- tected from birds (usu. end of January); see mwaka, kîmera. [see ya]

(111), 1. strong voice; īta műndğ na ~, call sb. by name loudly while he is still far away; ka na ~, come quickly, at once (before the echo fades). 2. used by old men to announce approach. 3. (cl. 6) power (primarily of voice), vitality, gaiety; kīria may, outdo, outshine, be too strong for. kayŭ, n. echo, distant voice.
